On the night of August 26, sacred chopsticks made from [[:en:Miscanthus sinensis|Japanese pampas grass]] are offered at the altar to pray for children's healthy growth, household safety, and prosperous business. On the morning of August 27, eating red bean porridge with these sacred chopsticks is believed to bring health and protection for the year.
== Cultural Properties ==
; Designated Tangible Cultural Property of Nagano City * **Large Ema Painting of the Onbashira Festival Procession** - Painted in 1860 (Man'en 1). Although the shrine was destroyed in the Zenkoji Earthquake, this large votive plaque depicts the Onbashira Festival procession held during the shrine’s reconstruction 13 years later.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Nagano City Cultural Property Database |url=http://bunkazai-nagano.jp/modules/dbsearch/page1176.html |website=bunkazai-nagano.jp |access-date=2023-03-12}}</ref>

[[sn:E6%AD%A6%E4%BA%95%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE]] [[sn:Takei Shrine]] [[ja:武井神社]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Nagano]] [[Category:Son-sha in Nagano Prefecture]] [[Category:qq]][[en:Takei Shrine]][[Category:qq]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis-->

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[d:Q11545788]] Wikidata name: Takei Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:29, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:46, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | Name=Takei Shrine | image=File:Takei Shrine.jpg | location=[[:en:Nagano (city)|Nagano, Nagano]], [[:en:Nagano Prefecture|Nagano Prefecture]], Higashimachi 191 | coordinates={{Coord|36.6565194|N|138.1895556|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deity={{ill|Takeminakata|qid=Q9150391}} | Status=Village shrine | Main Hall= | Alternate Names= | Annual Festival= | prefecture={{ill|Nagano|qid=Q11654093}} }}
'''Takei Shrine''' (武井神社) is located in {{ill|Higashimachi, Nagano|ja|長野市東町|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashimachi, Nagano|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, [[:en:Nagano (city)|Nagano City]], [[:en:Nagano Prefecture|Nagano Prefecture]]. Along with {{ill|Tsuma Shrine|ja|妻科神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuma Shrine|qq=}} and {{ill|Yufuku Shrine|ja|湯福神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yufuku Shrine|qq=|1=Yubuku Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11563772}}, it is known as one of the "Three Shrines of Zenkoji" (善光寺三社, also called the "Three Guardian Shrines of Zenkoji"). The exact founding date is unclear, but oral tradition suggests it may date back to the 5th year of Empress Jitō's reign (692 CE). The two deities enshrined together were added later.
== Overview ==
The origin of the shrine's name is explained in two theories: one from the {{ill|Shinano Hōkan|ja|信濃宝鑑|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinano Hōkan|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which states that the land belonging to [[:en:Suwa-taisha|Suwa-taisha]] was called "Takei" (or "Takei" written as 武居), and since the same deities were enshrined, it became known as Takei Shrine. Another theory claims that the shrine was established by the Takei Hōri clan (武居祝, pronounced as "Takei Hōri"), a family of Shinto priests. In ancient times, the shrine was referred to as Takei Myōjin or Suwa Myōjin. The current name was adopted in the 4th year of the Bunka era (1807).
The area where Takei Shrine is located was referred to as "Takei" in old maps, with the road to the south labeled as "Takei Alley" (武井小路).
There used to be an associated shrine to the north, known as Takei Ebisu Shrine, which is now [[:en:Nishinomiya Shrine|Nishinomiya Shrine]].<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|title=Nagano City History Vol. 8 |date=1997-10-16 |publisher=Tokyo Hōrei Publishing |page=42}}</ref>
== Shrine Grounds ==
The shrine grounds were expanded in 1897 (Meiji 30).<ref name=":0" />
=== Worship Hall ===
The current worship hall (拝殿) was rebuilt in 2009 (Heisei 21) as part of the commemorative project for the Onbashira Festival held the following year. The previous worship hall was constructed 13 years after the shrine buildings were destroyed in the major earthquake of 1847 (Kōka 4).<ref name=":0" />
[[File:武井神社 雷電の力石.jpg|thumb|Raiden's Power Stone]]
=== Raiden's Power Stone ===
According to tradition, there used to be a river southeast of the shrine with a bridge called Takei Bridge. When people struggled to move the heavy stone bridge, the famous sumo wrestler {{ill|Raiden Tameemon|ja|雷電為右衛門|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Raiden Tameemon|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 雷電爲右エ門|1=Raiden Tameemon|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q707985}}, who was on tour in Zenkoji, happened to pass by and moved the stone with ease.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Legends of the Temple Town |date=2003-04-10 |publisher=Ryūhō Shobō |pages=131-133}}</ref> It is also said that if children stand on this stone, they will grow up healthy (as noted on the shrine’s information board).
[[File:矢島稲荷社 武井神社.jpg|thumb|Yajima Inari Shrine]]
=== Yajima Inari Shrine ===
Long ago, a blind man named Yajima Ichirōbee lived in Iwaki Town, north of the shrine. Every night when he went to practice the {{ill|biwa|ja|琵琶|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/biwa|qq=|1=East Asian necked lute|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8084116}}, someone would kindly guide him. One day, he discovered the corpse of a fox and realized that the person who had been helping him was, in fact, a fox. Ichirōbee respectfully buried the fox in his garden and enshrined it by building an {{ill|Inari Shrine|ja|稲荷社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inari Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 稲荷神社|1=Inari shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q514480}}. This happened in 1800 (Kansei 12). The shrine was later relocated to the grounds of Takei Shrine in 1896 (Meiji 29) and is now known as Yajima Inari Shrine.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Legends of the Temple Town |date=2003-04-10 |publisher=Ryūhō Shobō |pages=128-130}}</ref>
=== Matsuo Shrine ===
=== Konpira Shrine, Mitsumine Shrine, and Tenjin Shrine ===
=== Sarutahiko Ōkami Monument === <gallery> File:松尾社（武井神社）.jpg|Matsuo Shrine File:金毘羅社・三峰社・天神社（武井神社）.jpg|Konpira Shrine, Mitsumine Shrine, Tenjin Shrine File:猿田彦大神の塚（武井神社）.jpg|Sarutahiko Ōkami Monument </gallery>
== Rituals ==
=== Onbashira Festival === {{main|Onbashira Festival}} Held in the years of the Tiger and the Monkey, similar to the Onbashira Festival of Suwa Taisha. The shrines of Zenkoji—Takei Shrine, Tsuma Shrine, Yufuku Shrine—along with {{ill|Minochi Taisha|ja|水内大社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minochi Taisha|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, take turns hosting the festival.
=== Misayama Festival ===
On the night of August 26, sacred chopsticks made from [[:en:Miscanthus sinensis|Japanese pampas grass]] are offered at the altar to pray for children's healthy growth, household safety, and prosperous business. On the morning of August 27, eating red bean porridge with these sacred chopsticks is believed to bring health and protection for the year.
== Cultural Properties ==
; Designated Tangible Cultural Property of Nagano City * **Large Ema Painting of the Onbashira Festival Procession** - Painted in 1860 (Man'en 1). Although the shrine was destroyed in the Zenkoji Earthquake, this large votive plaque depicts the Onbashira Festival procession held during the shrine’s reconstruction 13 years later.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Nagano City Cultural Property Database |url=http://bunkazai-nagano.jp/modules/dbsearch/page1176.html |website=bunkazai-nagano.jp |access-date=2023-03-12}}</ref>

=== Raiden's Power Stone ===

When people struggled to move the heavy stone bridge, the famous sumo wrestler {{ill|Raiden Tameemon|ja|雷電為右衛門|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Raiden Tameemon|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 雷電爲右エ門|1=Raiden Tameemon|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q707985}}, who was on tour in Zenkoji, happened to pass by and moved the stone with ease.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Legends of the Temple Town |date=2003-04-10 |publisher=Ryūhō Shobō |pages=131-133}}</ref> It is also said that if children stand on this stone, they will grow up healthy (as noted on the shrine’s information board).
[[File:矢島稲荷社 武井神社.jpg|thumb|Yajima Inari Shrine]]
=== Yajima Inari Shrine ===
Long ago, a blind man named Yajima Ichirōbee lived in Iwaki Town, north of the shrine. Every night when he went to practice the {{ill|biwa|ja|琵琶|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/biwa|qq=|1=pipa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6685124}}, someone would kindly guide him. One day, he discovered the corpse of a fox and realized that the person who had been helping him was, in fact, a fox. Ichirōbee respectfully buried the fox in his garden and enshrined it by building an {{ill|Inari Shrine|ja|稲荷社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inari Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 稲荷神社|1=Inari shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q514480}}. This happened in 1800 (Kansei 12). The shrine was later relocated to the grounds of Takei Shrine in 1896 (Meiji 29) and is now known as Yajima Inari Shrine.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Legends of the Temple Town |date=2003-04-10 |publisher=Ryūhō Shobō |pages=128-130}}</ref>
=== Matsuo Shrine ===
=== Konpira Shrine, Mitsumine Shrine, and Tenjin Shrine ===
=== Sarutahiko Ōkami Monument === <gallery> File:松尾社（武井神社）.jpg|Matsuo Shrine File:金毘羅社・三峰社・天神社（武井神社）.jpg|Konpira Shrine, Mitsumine Shrine, Tenjin Shrine File:猿田彦大神の塚（武井神社）.jpg|Sarutahiko Ōkami Monument </gallery>
== Rituals ==
=== Onbashira Festival === {{main|Onbashira Festival}} Held in the years of the Tiger and the Monkey, similar to the Onbashira Festival of Suwa Taisha. The shrines of Zenkoji—Takei Shrine, Tsuma Shrine, Yufuku Shrine—along with {{ill|Minochi Taisha|ja|水内大社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minochi Taisha|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, take turns hosting the festival.
=== Misayama Festival ===
On the night of August 26, sacred chopsticks made from [[:en:Miscanthus sinensis|Japanese pampas grass]] are offered at the altar to pray for children's healthy growth, household safety, and prosperous business. On the morning of August 27, eating red bean porridge with these sacred chopsticks is believed to bring health and protection for the year.
== Cultural Properties ==
; Designated Tangible Cultural Property of Nagano City * **Large Ema Painting of the Onbashira Festival Procession** - Painted in 1860 (Man'en 1). Although the shrine was destroyed in the Zenkoji Earthquake, this large votive plaque depicts the Onbashira Festival procession held during the shrine’s reconstruction 13 years later.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Nagano City Cultural Property Database |url=http://bunkazai-nagano.jp/modules/dbsearch/page1176.html |website=bunkazai-nagano.jp |access-date=2023-03-12}}</ref>
== References == {{Reflist}} == See Also == * [[:en:List of Shinto shrines in Japan|List of Shinto shrines in Japan]]

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[d:Q11545788]] Wikidata name: Takei Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:29, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:46, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | Name=Takei Shrine | image=File:Takei Shrine.jpg | location=[[:en:Nagano (city)|Nagano, Nagano]], [[:en:Nagano Prefecture|Nagano Prefecture]], Higashimachi 191 | coordinates={{Coord|36.6565194|N|138.1895556|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deity={{ill|Takeminakata|qid=Q9150391}} | Status=Village shrine | Main Hall= | Alternate Names= | Annual Festival= | prefecture={{ill|Nagano|qid=Q11654093}} }}
'''Takei Shrine''' (武井神社) is located in {{ill|Higashimachi, Nagano|ja|長野市東町|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashimachi, Nagano|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, [[:en:Nagano (city)|Nagano City]], [[:en:Nagano Prefecture|Nagano Prefecture]]. Along with {{ill|Tsuma Shrine|ja|妻科神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuma Shrine|qq=}} and {{ill|Yufuku Shrine|ja|湯福神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yufuku Shrine|qq=|1=Yubuku Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11563772}}, it is known as one of the "Three Shrines of Zenkoji" (善光寺三社, also called the "Three Guardian Shrines of Zenkoji"). The exact founding date is unclear, but oral tradition suggests it may date back to the 5th year of Empress Jitō's reign (692 CE). The two deities enshrined together were added later.
== Overview ==
The origin of the shrine's name is explained in two theories: one from the {{ill|Shinano Hōkan|ja|信濃宝鑑|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinano Hōkan|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which states that the land belonging to [[:en:Suwa-taisha|Suwa-taisha]] was called "Takei" (or "Takei" written as 武居), and since the same deities were enshrined, it became known as Takei Shrine. Another theory claims that the shrine was established by the Takei Hōri clan (武居祝, pronounced as "Takei Hōri"), a family of Shinto priests. In ancient times, the shrine was referred to as Takei Myōjin or Suwa Myōjin. The current name was adopted in the 4th year of the Bunka era (1807).
The area where Takei Shrine is located was referred to as "Takei" in old maps, with the road to the south labeled as "Takei Alley" (武井小路).
There used to be an associated shrine to the north, known as Takei Ebisu Shrine, which is now [[:en:Nishinomiya Shrine|Nishinomiya Shrine]].<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|title=Nagano City History Vol. 8 |date=1997-10-16 |publisher=Tokyo Hōrei Publishing |page=42}}</ref>
== Shrine Grounds ==
The shrine grounds were expanded in 1897 (Meiji 30).<ref name=":0" />
=== Worship Hall ===
The current worship hall (拝殿) was rebuilt in 2009 (Heisei 21) as part of the commemorative project for the Onbashira Festival held the following year. The previous worship hall was constructed 13 years after the shrine buildings were destroyed in the major earthquake of 1847 (Kōka 4).<ref name=":0" />
[[File:武井神社 雷電の力石.jpg|thumb|Raiden's Power Stone]]
=== Raiden's Power Stone ===
According to tradition, there used to be a river southeast of the shrine with a bridge called Takei Bridge. When people struggled to move the heavy stone bridge, the famous sumo wrestler {{ill|Raiden Tameemon|ja|雷電為右衛門|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Raiden Tameemon|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 雷電爲右エ門|1=Raiden Tameemon|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q707985}}, who was on tour in Zenkoji, happened to pass by and moved the stone with ease.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Legends of the Temple Town |date=2003-04-10 |publisher=Ryūhō Shobō |pages=131-133}}</ref> It is also said that if children stand on this stone, they will grow up healthy (as noted on the shrine’s information board).
[[File:矢島稲荷社 武井神社.jpg|thumb|Yajima Inari Shrine]]
=== Yajima Inari Shrine ===
Long ago, a blind man named Yajima Ichirōbee lived in Iwaki Town, north of the shrine. Every night when he went to practice the {{ill|biwa|ja|琵琶|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/biwa|qq=|1=pipa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6685124}}, someone would kindly guide him. One day, he discovered the corpse of a fox and realized that the person who had been helping him was, in fact, a fox. Ichirōbee respectfully buried the fox in his garden and enshrined it by building an {{ill|Inari Shrine|ja|稲荷社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inari Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 稲荷神社|1=Inari shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q514480}}. This happened in 1800 (Kansei 12). The shrine was later relocated to the grounds of Takei Shrine in 1896 (Meiji 29) and is now known as Yajima Inari Shrine.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Legends of the Temple Town |date=2003-04-10 |publisher=Ryūhō Shobō |pages=128-130}}</ref>
=== Matsuo Shrine ===
=== Konpira Shrine, Mitsumine Shrine, and Tenjin Shrine ===
=== Sarutahiko Ōkami Monument === <gallery> File:松尾社（武井神社）.jpg|Matsuo Shrine File:金毘羅社・三峰社・天神社（武井神社）.jpg|Konpira Shrine, Mitsumine Shrine, Tenjin Shrine File:猿田彦大神の塚（武井神社）.jpg|Sarutahiko Ōkami Monument </gallery>
== Rituals ==
=== Onbashira Festival === {{main|Onbashira Festival}} Held in the years of the Tiger and the Monkey, similar to the Onbashira Festival of Suwa Taisha. The shrines of Zenkoji—Takei Shrine, Tsuma Shrine, Yufuku Shrine—along with {{ill|Minochi Taisha|ja|水内大社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minochi Taisha|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, take turns hosting the festival.
=== Misayama Festival ===
On the night of August 26, sacred chopsticks made from [[:en:Miscanthus sinensis|Japanese pampas grass]] are offered at the altar to pray for children's healthy growth, household safety, and prosperous business. On the morning of August 27, eating red bean porridge with these sacred chopsticks is believed to bring health and protection for the year.
== Cultural Properties ==
; Designated Tangible Cultural Property of Nagano City * **Large Ema Painting of the Onbashira Festival Procession** - Painted in 1860 (Man'en 1). Although the shrine was destroyed in the Zenkoji Earthquake, this large votive plaque depicts the Onbashira Festival procession held during the shrine’s reconstruction 13 years later.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Nagano City Cultural Property Database |url=http://bunkazai-nagano.jp/modules/dbsearch/page1176.html |website=bunkazai-nagano.jp |access-date=2023-03-12}}</ref>
== References == {{Reflist}} == See Also == * [[:en:List of Shinto shrines in Japan|List of Shinto shrines in Japan]]
; Seven Shrines of Zenkoji : ※ indicates the Three Guardian Shrines of Zenkoji (善光寺三鎮守, Zenkoji Sansha) * {{ill|Miwa Shrine (Nagano)|ja|美和神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miwa Shrine (Nagano)|qq=}} * {{ill|Yufuku Shrine|ja|湯福神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yufuku Shrine|qq=|1=Yubuku Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11563772}} ※ * '''Takei Shrine''' ※ * {{ill|Tsuma Shrine|ja|妻科神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuma Shrine|qq=}} ※ * {{ill|Kamo Shrine (Nagano)|ja|加茂神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamo Shrine (Nagano)|qq=|1=Kamo Shrine (disambiguation)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11398885}} * {{ill|Kidome Shrine|ja|木留神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kidome Shrine|qq=|1=Kitome Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11519306}} * {{ill|Yanagihara Shrine (Nagano)|ja|柳原神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yanagihara Shrine (Nagano)|qq=|1=Yanagihara Shrine (Nagano)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q131226981}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:武井神社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[d:Q11545788]] Wikidata name: Takei Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:29, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:46, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | Name=Takei Shrine | image=File:Takei Shrine.jpg | location=[[:en:Nagano (city)|Nagano, Nagano]], [[:en:Nagano Prefecture|Nagano Prefecture]], Higashimachi 191 | coordinates={{Coord|36.6565194|N|138.1895556|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deity={{ill|Takeminakata|qid=Q9150391}} | Status=Village shrine | Main Hall= | Alternate Names= | Annual Festival= | prefecture={{ill|Nagano|qid=Q11654093}} }}
'''Takei Shrine''' (武井神社) is located in {{ill|Higashimachi, Nagano|ja|長野市東町|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashimachi, Nagano|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, [[:en:Nagano (city)|Nagano City]], [[:en:Nagano Prefecture|Nagano Prefecture]]. Along with {{ill|Tsuma Shrine|ja|妻科神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuma Shrine|qq=}} and {{ill|Yufuku Shrine|ja|湯福神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yufuku Shrine|qq=|1=Yubuku Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11563772}}, it is known as one of the "Three Shrines of Zenkoji" (善光寺三社, also called the "Three Guardian Shrines of Zenkoji"). The exact founding date is unclear, but oral tradition suggests it may date back to the 5th year of Empress Jitō's reign (692 CE). The two deities enshrined together were added later.
== Overview ==
The origin of the shrine's name is explained in two theories: one from the {{ill|Shinano Hōkan|ja|信濃宝鑑|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinano Hōkan|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which states that the land belonging to [[:en:Suwa-taisha|Suwa-taisha]] was called "Takei" (or "Takei" written as 武居), and since the same deities were enshrined, it became known as Takei Shrine. Another theory claims that the shrine was established by the Takei Hōri clan (武居祝, pronounced as "Takei Hōri"), a family of Shinto priests. In ancient times, the shrine was referred to as Takei Myōjin or Suwa Myōjin. The current name was adopted in the 4th year of the Bunka era (1807).
The area where Takei Shrine is located was referred to as "Takei" in old maps, with the road to the south labeled as "Takei Alley" (武井小路).
There used to be an associated shrine to the north, known as Takei Ebisu Shrine, which is now [[:en:Nishinomiya Shrine|Nishinomiya Shrine]].<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|title=Nagano City History Vol. 8 |date=1997-10-16 |publisher=Tokyo Hōrei Publishing |page=42}}</ref>
== Shrine Grounds ==
The shrine grounds were expanded in 1897 (Meiji 30).<ref name=":0" />
=== Worship Hall ===
The current worship hall (拝殿) was rebuilt in 2009 (Heisei 21) as part of the commemorative project for the Onbashira Festival held the following year. The previous worship hall was constructed 13 years after the shrine buildings were destroyed in the major earthquake of 1847 (Kōka 4).<ref name=":0" />
[[File:武井神社 雷電の力石.jpg|thumb|Raiden's Power Stone]]
=== Raiden's Power Stone ===
According to tradition, there used to be a river southeast of the shrine with a bridge called Takei Bridge. When people struggled to move the heavy stone bridge, the famous sumo wrestler {{ill|Raiden Tameemon|ja|雷電為右衛門|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Raiden Tameemon|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 雷電爲右エ門|1=Raiden Tameemon|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q707985}}, who was on tour in Zenkoji, happened to pass by and moved the stone with ease.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Legends of the Temple Town |date=2003-04-10 |publisher=Ryūhō Shobō |pages=131-133}}</ref> It is also said that if children stand on this stone, they will grow up healthy (as noted on the shrine’s information board).
[[File:矢島稲荷社 武井神社.jpg|thumb|Yajima Inari Shrine]]
=== Yajima Inari Shrine ===
Long ago, a blind man named Yajima Ichirōbee lived in Iwaki Town, north of the shrine. Every night when he went to practice the {{ill|biwa|ja|琵琶|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/biwa|qq=|1=pipa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6685124}}, someone would kindly guide him. One day, he discovered the corpse of a fox and realized that the person who had been helping him was, in fact, a fox. Ichirōbee respectfully buried the fox in his garden and enshrined it by building an {{ill|Inari Shrine|ja|稲荷社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inari Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 稲荷神社|1=Inari shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q514480}}. This happened in 1800 (Kansei 12). The shrine was later relocated to the grounds of Takei Shrine in 1896 (Meiji 29) and is now known as Yajima Inari Shrine.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Legends of the Temple Town |date=2003-04-10 |publisher=Ryūhō Shobō |pages=128-130}}</ref>
=== Matsuo Shrine ===
=== Konpira Shrine, Mitsumine Shrine, and Tenjin Shrine ===
=== Sarutahiko Ōkami Monument === <gallery> File:松尾社（武井神社）.jpg|Matsuo Shrine File:金毘羅社・三峰社・天神社（武井神社）.jpg|Konpira Shrine, Mitsumine Shrine, Tenjin Shrine File:猿田彦大神の塚（武井神社）.jpg|Sarutahiko Ōkami Monument </gallery>
== Rituals ==
=== Onbashira Festival === {{main|Onbashira Festival}} Held in the years of the Tiger and the Monkey, similar to the Onbashira Festival of Suwa Taisha. The shrines of Zenkoji—Takei Shrine, Tsuma Shrine, Yufuku Shrine—along with {{ill|Minochi Taisha|ja|水内大社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minochi Taisha|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, take turns hosting the festival.
=== Misayama Festival ===
On the night of August 26, sacred chopsticks made from [[:en:Miscanthus sinensis|Japanese pampas grass]] are offered at the altar to pray for children's healthy growth, household safety, and prosperous business. On the morning of August 27, eating red bean porridge with these sacred chopsticks is believed to bring health and protection for the year.
== Cultural Properties ==
; Designated Tangible Cultural Property of Nagano City * **Large Ema Painting of the Onbashira Festival Procession** - Painted in 1860 (Man'en 1). Although the shrine was destroyed in the Zenkoji Earthquake, this large votive plaque depicts the Onbashira Festival procession held during the shrine’s reconstruction 13 years later.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Nagano City Cultural Property Database |url=http://bunkazai-nagano.jp/modules/dbsearch/page1176.html |website=bunkazai-nagano.jp |access-date=2023-03-12}}</ref>

On the night of August 26, sacred chopsticks made from [[:en:Miscanthus sinensis|Japanese pampas grass]] are offered at the altar to pray for children's healthy growth, household safety, and prosperous business. On the morning of August 27, eating red bean porridge with these sacred chopsticks is believed to bring health and protection for the year.
== Cultural Properties ==
; Designated Tangible Cultural Property of Nagano City * **Large Ema Painting of the Onbashira Festival Procession** - Painted in 1860 (Man'en 1). Although the shrine was destroyed in the Zenkoji Earthquake, this large votive plaque depicts the Onbashira Festival procession held during the shrine’s reconstruction 13 years later.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Nagano City Cultural Property Database |url=http://bunkazai-nagano.jp/modules/dbsearch/page1176.html |website=bunkazai-nagano.jp |access-date=2023-03-12}}</ref>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:武井神社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[da:d:Q11545788]] Wikidata name: Takei Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:29, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:46, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | Name=Takei Shrine | image=File:Takei Shrine.jpg | location=[[:en:Nagano (city)|Nagano, Nagano]], [[:en:Nagano Prefecture|Nagano Prefecture]], Higashimachi 191 | coordinates={{Coord|36.6565194|N|138.1895556|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deity={{ill|Takeminakata|WD=Q9150391}} | Status=Village shrine | Main Hall= | Alternate Names= | Annual Festival= | prefecture={{ill|Nagano|WD=Q11654093}} }}
'''Takei Shrine''' (武井神社) is located in {{ill|Higashimachi, Nagano|ja|長野市東町|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashimachi, Nagano|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, [[:en:Nagano (city)|Nagano City]], [[:en:Nagano Prefecture|Nagano Prefecture]]. Along with {{ill|Tsuma Shrine|ja|妻科神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuma Shrine|qq=}} and {{ill|Yufuku Shrine|ja|湯福神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yufuku Shrine|1=Yubuku Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11563772}}, it is known as one of the "Three Shrines of Zenkoji" (善光寺三社, also called the "Three Guardian Shrines of Zenkoji"). The exact founding date is unclear, but oral tradition suggests it may date back to the 5th year of Empress Jitō's reign (692 CE). The two deities enshrined together were added later.
== Overview ==
The origin of the shrine's name is explained in two theories: one from the {{ill|Shinano Hōkan|ja|信濃宝鑑|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinano Hōkan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which states that the land belonging to [[:en:Suwa-taisha|Suwa-taisha]] was called "Takei" (or "Takei" written as 武居), and since the same deities were enshrined, it became known as Takei Shrine. Another theory claims that the shrine was established by the Takei Hōri clan (武居祝, pronounced as "Takei Hōri"), a family of Shinto priests. In ancient times, the shrine was referred to as Takei Myōjin or Suwa Myōjin. The current name was adopted in the 4th year of the Bunka era (1807).
The area where Takei Shrine is located was referred to as "Takei" in old maps, with the road to the south labeled as "Takei Alley" (武井小路).
There used to be an associated shrine to the north, known as Takei Ebisu Shrine, which is now [[:en:Nishinomiya Shrine|Nishinomiya Shrine]].<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|title=Nagano City History Vol. 8 |date=1997-10-16 |publisher=Tokyo Hōrei Publishing |page=42}}</ref>
== Shrine Grounds ==
The shrine grounds were expanded in 1897 (Meiji 30).<ref name=":0" />
=== Worship Hall ===
The current worship hall (拝殿) was rebuilt in 2009 (Heisei 21) as part of the commemorative project for the Onbashira Festival held the following year. The previous worship hall was constructed 13 years after the shrine buildings were destroyed in the major earthquake of 1847 (Kōka 4).<ref name=":0" />
[[File:武井神社 雷電の力石.jpg|thumb|Raiden's Power Stone]]
=== Raiden's Power Stone ===
According to tradition, there used to be a river southeast of the shrine with a bridge called Takei Bridge. When people struggled to move the heavy stone bridge, the famous sumo wrestler {{ill|Raiden Tameemon|ja|雷電為右衛門|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Raiden Tameemon|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 雷電爲右エ門|1=Raiden Tameemon|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q707985}}, who was on tour in Zenkoji, happened to pass by and moved the stone with ease.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Legends of the Temple Town |date=2003-04-10 |publisher=Ryūhō Shobō |pages=131-133}}</ref> It is also said that if children stand on this stone, they will grow up healthy (as noted on the shrine’s information board).
[[File:矢島稲荷社 武井神社.jpg|thumb|Yajima Inari Shrine]]
=== Yajima Inari Shrine ===
Long ago, a blind man named Yajima Ichirōbee lived in Iwaki Town, north of the shrine. Every night when he went to practice the {{ill|biwa|ja|琵琶|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/biwa|1=pipa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6685124}}, someone would kindly guide him. One day, he discovered the corpse of a fox and realized that the person who had been helping him was, in fact, a fox. Ichirōbee respectfully buried the fox in his garden and enshrined it by building an {{ill|Inari Shrine|ja|稲荷社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inari Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 稲荷神社|1=Inari shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q514480}}. This happened in 1800 (Kansei 12). The shrine was later relocated to the grounds of Takei Shrine in 1896 (Meiji 29) and is now known as Yajima Inari Shrine.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Legends of the Temple Town |date=2003-04-10 |publisher=Ryūhō Shobō |pages=128-130}}</ref>
=== Matsuo Shrine ===
=== Konpira Shrine, Mitsumine Shrine, and Tenjin Shrine ===
=== Sarutahiko Ōkami Monument === <gallery> File:松尾社（武井神社）.jpg|Matsuo Shrine File:金毘羅社・三峰社・天神社（武井神社）.jpg|Konpira Shrine, Mitsumine Shrine, Tenjin Shrine File:猿田彦大神の塚（武井神社）.jpg|Sarutahiko Ōkami Monument </gallery>
== Rituals ==
=== Onbashira Festival === {{main|Onbashira Festival}} Held in the years of the Tiger and the Monkey, similar to the Onbashira Festival of Suwa Taisha. The shrines of Zenkoji—Takei Shrine, Tsuma Shrine, Yufuku Shrine—along with {{ill|Minochi Taisha|ja|水内大社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minochi Taisha|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, take turns hosting the festival.
=== Misayama Festival ===
On the night of August 26, sacred chopsticks made from [[:en:Miscanthus sinensis|Japanese pampas grass]] are offered at the altar to pray for children's healthy growth, household safety, and prosperous business. On the morning of August 27, eating red bean porridge with these sacred chopsticks is believed to bring health and protection for the year.
== Cultural Properties ==
; Designated Tangible Cultural Property of Nagano City * **Large Ema Painting of the Onbashira Festival Procession** - Painted in 1860 (Man'en 1). Although the shrine was destroyed in the Zenkoji Earthquake, this large votive plaque depicts the Onbashira Festival procession held during the shrine’s reconstruction 13 years later.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Nagano City Cultural Property Database |url=http://bunkazai-nagano.jp/modules/dbsearch/page1176.html |website=bunkazai-nagano.jp |access-date=2023-03-12}}</ref>

=== Raiden's Power Stone ===

When people struggled to move the heavy stone bridge, the famous sumo wrestler {{ill|Raiden Tameemon|ja|雷電為右衛門|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Raiden Tameemon|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 雷電爲右エ門|1=Raiden Tameemon|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q707985}}, who was on tour in Zenkoji, happened to pass by and moved the stone with ease.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Legends of the Temple Town |date=2003-04-10 |publisher=Ryūhō Shobō |pages=131-133}}</ref> It is also said that if children stand on this stone, they will grow up healthy (as noted on the shrine’s information board).
[[File:矢島稲荷社 武井神社.jpg|thumb|Yajima Inari Shrine]]
=== Yajima Inari Shrine ===
Long ago, a blind man named Yajima Ichirōbee lived in Iwaki Town, north of the shrine. Every night when he went to practice the {{ill|biwa|ja|琵琶|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/biwa|1=pipa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6685124}}, someone would kindly guide him. One day, he discovered the corpse of a fox and realized that the person who had been helping him was, in fact, a fox. Ichirōbee respectfully buried the fox in his garden and enshrined it by building an {{ill|Inari Shrine|ja|稲荷社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inari Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 稲荷神社|1=Inari shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q514480}}. This happened in 1800 (Kansei 12). The shrine was later relocated to the grounds of Takei Shrine in 1896 (Meiji 29) and is now known as Yajima Inari Shrine.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Legends of the Temple Town |date=2003-04-10 |publisher=Ryūhō Shobō |pages=128-130}}</ref>
=== Matsuo Shrine ===
=== Konpira Shrine, Mitsumine Shrine, and Tenjin Shrine ===
=== Sarutahiko Ōkami Monument === <gallery> File:松尾社（武井神社）.jpg|Matsuo Shrine File:金毘羅社・三峰社・天神社（武井神社）.jpg|Konpira Shrine, Mitsumine Shrine, Tenjin Shrine File:猿田彦大神の塚（武井神社）.jpg|Sarutahiko Ōkami Monument </gallery>
== Rituals ==
=== Onbashira Festival === {{main|Onbashira Festival}} Held in the years of the Tiger and the Monkey, similar to the Onbashira Festival of Suwa Taisha. The shrines of Zenkoji—Takei Shrine, Tsuma Shrine, Yufuku Shrine—along with {{ill|Minochi Taisha|ja|水内大社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minochi Taisha|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, take turns hosting the festival.
=== Misayama Festival ===
On the night of August 26, sacred chopsticks made from [[:en:Miscanthus sinensis|Japanese pampas grass]] are offered at the altar to pray for children's healthy growth, household safety, and prosperous business. On the morning of August 27, eating red bean porridge with these sacred chopsticks is believed to bring health and protection for the year.
== Cultural Properties ==
; Designated Tangible Cultural Property of Nagano City * **Large Ema Painting of the Onbashira Festival Procession** - Painted in 1860 (Man'en 1). Although the shrine was destroyed in the Zenkoji Earthquake, this large votive plaque depicts the Onbashira Festival procession held during the shrine’s reconstruction 13 years later.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Nagano City Cultural Property Database |url=http://bunkazai-nagano.jp/modules/dbsearch/page1176.html |website=bunkazai-nagano.jp |access-date=2023-03-12}}</ref>
== References == {{Reflist}} == See Also == * [[:en:List of Shinto shrines in Japan|List of Shinto shrines in Japan]]
; Seven Shrines of Zenkoji : ※ indicates the Three Guardian Shrines of Zenkoji (善光寺三鎮守, Zenkoji Sansha) * {{ill|Miwa Shrine (Nagano)|ja|美和神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miwa Shrine (Nagano)|qq=}} * {{ill|Yufuku Shrine|ja|湯福神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yufuku Shrine|1=Yubuku Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11563772}} ※ * '''Takei Shrine''' ※ * {{ill|Tsuma Shrine|ja|妻科神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuma Shrine|qq=}} ※ * {{ill|Kamo Shrine (Nagano)|ja|加茂神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamo Shrine (Nagano)|1=Kamo Shrine (disambiguation)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11398885}} * {{ill|Kidome Shrine|ja|木留神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kidome Shrine|1=Kitome Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11519306}} * {{ill|Yanagihara Shrine (Nagano)|ja|柳原神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yanagihara Shrine (Nagano)|1=Yanagihara Shrine (Nagano)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q131226981}}

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:武井神社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[da:d:Q11545788]] Wikidata name: Takei Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:29, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:46, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | Name=Takei Shrine | image=File:Takei Shrine.jpg | location=[[:en:Nagano (city)|Nagano, Nagano]], [[:en:Nagano Prefecture|Nagano Prefecture]], Higashimachi 191 | coordinates={{Coord|36.6565194|N|138.1895556|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deity={{ill|Takeminakata|WD=Q9150391}} | Status=Village shrine | Main Hall= | Alternate Names= | Annual Festival= | prefecture={{ill|Nagano|WD=Q11654093}} }}
'''Takei Shrine''' (武井神社) is located in {{ill|Higashimachi, Nagano|ja|長野市東町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashimachi, Nagano|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, [[:en:Nagano (city)|Nagano City]], [[:en:Nagano Prefecture|Nagano Prefecture]]. Along with {{ill|Tsuma Shrine|ja|妻科神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuma Shrine|qq=}} and {{ill|Yufuku Shrine|ja|湯福神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yufuku Shrine|1=Yubuku Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11563772}}, it is known as one of the "Three Shrines of Zenkoji" (善光寺三社, also called the "Three Guardian Shrines of Zenkoji"). The exact founding date is unclear, but oral tradition suggests it may date back to the 5th year of Empress Jitō's reign (692 CE). The two deities enshrined together were added later.
== Overview ==
The origin of the shrine's name is explained in two theories: one from the {{ill|Shinano Hōkan|ja|信濃宝鑑|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinano Hōkan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which states that the land belonging to [[:en:Suwa-taisha|Suwa-taisha]] was called "Takei" (or "Takei" written as 武居), and since the same deities were enshrined, it became known as Takei Shrine. Another theory claims that the shrine was established by the Takei Hōri clan (武居祝, pronounced as "Takei Hōri"), a family of Shinto priests. In ancient times, the shrine was referred to as Takei Myōjin or Suwa Myōjin. The current name was adopted in the 4th year of the Bunka era (1807).
The area where Takei Shrine is located was referred to as "Takei" in old maps, with the road to the south labeled as "Takei Alley" (武井小路).
There used to be an associated shrine to the north, known as Takei Ebisu Shrine, which is now [[:en:Nishinomiya Shrine|Nishinomiya Shrine]].<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|title=Nagano City History Vol. 8 |date=1997-10-16 |publisher=Tokyo Hōrei Publishing |page=42}}</ref>
== Shrine Grounds ==
The shrine grounds were expanded in 1897 (Meiji 30).<ref name=":0" />
=== Worship Hall ===
The current worship hall (拝殿) was rebuilt in 2009 (Heisei 21) as part of the commemorative project for the Onbashira Festival held the following year. The previous worship hall was constructed 13 years after the shrine buildings were destroyed in the major earthquake of 1847 (Kōka 4).<ref name=":0" />
[[File:武井神社 雷電の力石.jpg|thumb|Raiden's Power Stone]]
=== Raiden's Power Stone ===
According to tradition, there used to be a river southeast of the shrine with a bridge called Takei Bridge. When people struggled to move the heavy stone bridge, the famous sumo wrestler {{ill|Raiden Tameemon|ja|雷電為右衛門|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Raiden Tameemon|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 雷電爲右エ門|1=Raiden Tameemon|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q707985}}, who was on tour in Zenkoji, happened to pass by and moved the stone with ease.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Legends of the Temple Town |date=2003-04-10 |publisher=Ryūhō Shobō |pages=131-133}}</ref> It is also said that if children stand on this stone, they will grow up healthy (as noted on the shrine’s information board).
[[File:矢島稲荷社 武井神社.jpg|thumb|Yajima Inari Shrine]]
=== Yajima Inari Shrine ===
Long ago, a blind man named Yajima Ichirōbee lived in Iwaki Town, north of the shrine. Every night when he went to practice the {{ill|biwa|ja|琵琶|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/biwa|1=pipa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6685124}}, someone would kindly guide him. One day, he discovered the corpse of a fox and realized that the person who had been helping him was, in fact, a fox. Ichirōbee respectfully buried the fox in his garden and enshrined it by building an {{ill|Inari Shrine|ja|稲荷社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inari Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 稲荷神社|1=Inari shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q514480}}. This happened in 1800 (Kansei 12). The shrine was later relocated to the grounds of Takei Shrine in 1896 (Meiji 29) and is now known as Yajima Inari Shrine.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Legends of the Temple Town |date=2003-04-10 |publisher=Ryūhō Shobō |pages=128-130}}</ref>
=== Matsuo Shrine ===
=== Konpira Shrine, Mitsumine Shrine, and Tenjin Shrine ===
=== Sarutahiko Ōkami Monument === <gallery> File:松尾社（武井神社）.jpg|Matsuo Shrine File:金毘羅社・三峰社・天神社（武井神社）.jpg|Konpira Shrine, Mitsumine Shrine, Tenjin Shrine File:猿田彦大神の塚（武井神社）.jpg|Sarutahiko Ōkami Monument </gallery>

The shrines of Zenkoji—Takei Shrine, Tsuma Shrine, Yufuku Shrine—along with {{ill|Minochi Taisha|ja|水内大社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minochi Taisha|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, take turns hosting the festival.

On the night of August 26, sacred chopsticks made from [[:en:Miscanthus sinensis|Japanese pampas grass]] are offered at the altar to pray for children's healthy growth, household safety, and prosperous business. On the morning of August 27, eating red bean porridge with these sacred chopsticks is believed to bring health and protection for the year.
== Cultural Properties ==
; Designated Tangible Cultural Property of Nagano City * **Large Ema Painting of the Onbashira Festival Procession** - Painted in 1860 (Man'en 1). Although the shrine was destroyed in the Zenkoji Earthquake, this large votive plaque depicts the Onbashira Festival procession held during the shrine’s reconstruction 13 years later.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Nagano City Cultural Property Database |url=http://bunkazai-nagano.jp/modules/dbsearch/page1176.html |website=bunkazai-nagano.jp |access-date=2023-03-12}}</ref>

The exact founding date is unclear, but oral tradition suggests it may date back to the 5th year of Empress Jitō's reign (692 CE). The two deities enshrined together were added later.

The origin of the shrine's name is explained in two theories: one from the {{ill|Shinano Hōkan|ja|信濃宝鑑|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinano Hōkan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which states that the land belonging to [[:en:Suwa-taisha|Suwa-taisha]] was called "Takei" (or "Takei" written as 武居), and since the same deities were enshrined, it became known as Takei Shrine. Another theory claims that the shrine was established by the Takei Hōri clan (武居祝, pronounced as "Takei Hōri"), a family of Shinto priests. In ancient times, the shrine was referred to as Takei Myōjin or Suwa Myōjin. The current name was adopted in the 4th year of the Bunka era (1807).
The area where Takei Shrine is located was referred to as "Takei" in old maps, with the road to the south labeled as "Takei Alley" (武井小路).
There used to be an associated shrine to the north, known as Takei Ebisu Shrine, which is now [[:en:Nishinomiya Shrine|Nishinomiya Shrine]].<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|title=Nagano City History Vol. 8 |date=1997-10-16 |publisher=Tokyo Hōrei Publishing |page=42}}</ref>
== Shrine Grounds ==
The shrine grounds were expanded in 1897 (Meiji 30).<ref name=":0" />
=== Worship Hall ===
The current worship hall (拝殿) was rebuilt in 2009 (Heisei 21) as part of the commemorative project for the Onbashira Festival held the following year. The previous worship hall was constructed 13 years after the shrine buildings were destroyed in the major earthquake of 1847 (Kōka 4).<ref name=":0" />
[[File:武井神社 雷電の力石.jpg|thumb|Raiden's Power Stone]]
=== Raiden's Power Stone ===
According to tradition, there used to be a river southeast of the shrine with a bridge called Takei Bridge. When people struggled to move the heavy stone bridge, the famous sumo wrestler {{ill|Raiden Tameemon|ja|雷電為右衛門|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Raiden Tameemon|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 雷電爲右エ門|1=Raiden Tameemon|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q707985}}, who was on tour in Zenkoji, happened to pass by and moved the stone with ease.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Legends of the Temple Town |date=2003-04-10 |publisher=Ryūhō Shobō |pages=131-133}}</ref> It is also said that if children stand on this stone, they will grow up healthy (as noted on the shrine’s information board).
[[File:矢島稲荷社 武井神社.jpg|thumb|Yajima Inari Shrine]]
=== Yajima Inari Shrine ===
Long ago, a blind man named Yajima Ichirōbee lived in Iwaki Town, north of the shrine. Every night when he went to practice the {{ill|biwa|ja|琵琶|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/biwa|1=pipa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6685124}}, someone would kindly guide him. One day, he discovered the corpse of a fox and realized that the person who had been helping him was, in fact, a fox. Ichirōbee respectfully buried the fox in his garden and enshrined it by building an {{ill|Inari Shrine|ja|稲荷社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inari Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 稲荷神社|1=Inari shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q514480}}. This happened in 1800 (Kansei 12). The shrine was later relocated to the grounds of Takei Shrine in 1896 (Meiji 29) and is now known as Yajima Inari Shrine.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Legends of the Temple Town |date=2003-04-10 |publisher=Ryūhō Shobō |pages=128-130}}</ref>
=== Matsuo Shrine ===
=== Konpira Shrine, Mitsumine Shrine, and Tenjin Shrine ===
=== Sarutahiko Ōkami Monument === <gallery> File:松尾社（武井神社）.jpg|Matsuo Shrine File:金毘羅社・三峰社・天神社（武井神社）.jpg|Konpira Shrine, Mitsumine Shrine, Tenjin Shrine File:猿田彦大神の塚（武井神社）.jpg|Sarutahiko Ōkami Monument </gallery>
== Rituals ==
=== Onbashira Festival === {{main|Onbashira Festival}} Held in the years of the Tiger and the Monkey, similar to the Onbashira Festival of Suwa Taisha. The shrines of Zenkoji—Takei Shrine, Tsuma Shrine, Yufuku Shrine—along with {{ill|Minochi Taisha|ja|水内大社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minochi Taisha|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, take turns hosting the festival.
=== Misayama Festival ===
On the night of August 26, sacred chopsticks made from [[:en:Miscanthus sinensis|Japanese pampas grass]] are offered at the altar to pray for children's healthy growth, household safety, and prosperous business. On the morning of August 27, eating red bean porridge with these sacred chopsticks is believed to bring health and protection for the year.
== Cultural Properties ==
; Designated Tangible Cultural Property of Nagano City * **Large Ema Painting of the Onbashira Festival Procession** - Painted in 1860 (Man'en 1). Although the shrine was destroyed in the Zenkoji Earthquake, this large votive plaque depicts the Onbashira Festival procession held during the shrine’s reconstruction 13 years later.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Nagano City Cultural Property Database |url=http://bunkazai-nagano.jp/modules/dbsearch/page1176.html |website=bunkazai-nagano.jp |access-date=2023-03-12}}</ref>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:武井神社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[da:d:Q11545788]] Wikidata name: Takei Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:29, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:46, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | Name=Takei Shrine | image=File:Takei Shrine.jpg | location=[[:en:Nagano (city)|Nagano, Nagano]], [[:en:Nagano Prefecture|Nagano Prefecture]], Higashimachi 191 | coordinates={{Coord|36.6565194|N|138.1895556|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deity={{ill|Takeminakata|WD=Q9150391}} | Status=Village shrine | Main Hall= | Alternate Names= | Annual Festival= | prefecture={{ill|Nagano|WD=Q11654093}} }}
'''Takei Shrine''' (武井神社) is located in {{ill|Higashimachi, Nagano|ja|長野市東町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashimachi, Nagano|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, [[:en:Nagano (city)|Nagano City]], [[:en:Nagano Prefecture|Nagano Prefecture]]. Along with {{ill|Tsuma Shrine|ja|妻科神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuma Shrine|qq=}} and {{ill|Yufuku Shrine|ja|湯福神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yufuku Shrine|1=Yubuku Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11563772}}, it is known as one of the "Three Shrines of Zenkoji" (善光寺三社, also called the "Three Guardian Shrines of Zenkoji"). The exact founding date is unclear, but oral tradition suggests it may date back to the 5th year of Empress Jitō's reign (692 CE). The two deities enshrined together were added later.
== Overview ==
The origin of the shrine's name is explained in two theories: one from the {{ill|Shinano Hōkan|ja|信濃宝鑑|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinano Hōkan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which states that the land belonging to [[:en:Suwa-taisha|Suwa-taisha]] was called "Takei" (or "Takei" written as 武居), and since the same deities were enshrined, it became known as Takei Shrine. Another theory claims that the shrine was established by the Takei Hōri clan (武居祝, pronounced as "Takei Hōri"), a family of Shinto priests. In ancient times, the shrine was referred to as Takei Myōjin or Suwa Myōjin. The current name was adopted in the 4th year of the Bunka era (1807).
The area where Takei Shrine is located was referred to as "Takei" in old maps, with the road to the south labeled as "Takei Alley" (武井小路).
There used to be an associated shrine to the north, known as Takei Ebisu Shrine, which is now [[:en:Nishinomiya Shrine|Nishinomiya Shrine]].<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|title=Nagano City History Vol. 8 |date=1997-10-16 |publisher=Tokyo Hōrei Publishing |page=42}}</ref>
== Shrine Grounds ==
The shrine grounds were expanded in 1897 (Meiji 30).<ref name=":0" />
=== Worship Hall ===
The current worship hall (拝殿) was rebuilt in 2009 (Heisei 21) as part of the commemorative project for the Onbashira Festival held the following year. The previous worship hall was constructed 13 years after the shrine buildings were destroyed in the major earthquake of 1847 (Kōka 4).<ref name=":0" />
[[File:武井神社 雷電の力石.jpg|thumb|Raiden's Power Stone]]
=== Raiden's Power Stone ===
According to tradition, there used to be a river southeast of the shrine with a bridge called Takei Bridge. When people struggled to move the heavy stone bridge, the famous sumo wrestler {{ill|Raiden Tameemon|ja|雷電為右衛門|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Raiden Tameemon|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 雷電爲右エ門|1=Raiden Tameemon|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q707985}}, who was on tour in Zenkoji, happened to pass by and moved the stone with ease.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Legends of the Temple Town |date=2003-04-10 |publisher=Ryūhō Shobō |pages=131-133}}</ref> It is also said that if children stand on this stone, they will grow up healthy (as noted on the shrine’s information board).
[[File:矢島稲荷社 武井神社.jpg|thumb|Yajima Inari Shrine]]
=== Yajima Inari Shrine ===
Long ago, a blind man named Yajima Ichirōbee lived in Iwaki Town, north of the shrine. Every night when he went to practice the {{ill|biwa|ja|琵琶|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/biwa|1=pipa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6685124}}, someone would kindly guide him. One day, he discovered the corpse of a fox and realized that the person who had been helping him was, in fact, a fox. Ichirōbee respectfully buried the fox in his garden and enshrined it by building an {{ill|Inari Shrine|ja|稲荷社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inari Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 稲荷神社|1=Inari shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q514480}}. This happened in 1800 (Kansei 12). The shrine was later relocated to the grounds of Takei Shrine in 1896 (Meiji 29) and is now known as Yajima Inari Shrine.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Legends of the Temple Town |date=2003-04-10 |publisher=Ryūhō Shobō |pages=128-130}}</ref>
=== Matsuo Shrine ===
=== Konpira Shrine, Mitsumine Shrine, and Tenjin Shrine ===
=== Sarutahiko Ōkami Monument === <gallery> File:松尾社（武井神社）.jpg|Matsuo Shrine File:金毘羅社・三峰社・天神社（武井神社）.jpg|Konpira Shrine, Mitsumine Shrine, Tenjin Shrine File:猿田彦大神の塚（武井神社）.jpg|Sarutahiko Ōkami Monument </gallery>
== Rituals ==
=== Onbashira Festival === {{main|Onbashira Festival}} Held in the years of the Tiger and the Monkey, similar to the Onbashira Festival of Suwa Taisha. The shrines of Zenkoji—Takei Shrine, Tsuma Shrine, Yufuku Shrine—along with {{ill|Minochi Taisha|ja|水内大社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minochi Taisha|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, take turns hosting the festival.
=== Misayama Festival ===
On the night of August 26, sacred chopsticks made from [[:en:Miscanthus sinensis|Japanese pampas grass]] are offered at the altar to pray for children's healthy growth, household safety, and prosperous business. On the morning of August 27, eating red bean porridge with these sacred chopsticks is believed to bring health and protection for the year.
== Cultural Properties ==
; Designated Tangible Cultural Property of Nagano City * **Large Ema Painting of the Onbashira Festival Procession** - Painted in 1860 (Man'en 1). Although the shrine was destroyed in the Zenkoji Earthquake, this large votive plaque depicts the Onbashira Festival procession held during the shrine’s reconstruction 13 years later.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Nagano City Cultural Property Database |url=http://bunkazai-nagano.jp/modules/dbsearch/page1176.html |website=bunkazai-nagano.jp |access-date=2023-03-12}}</ref>
== References == {{Reflist}} == See Also == * [[:en:List of Shinto shrines in Japan|List of Shinto shrines in Japan]]
; Seven Shrines of Zenkoji : ※ indicates the Three Guardian Shrines of Zenkoji (善光寺三鎮守, Zenkoji Sansha) * {{ill|Miwa Shrine (Nagano)|ja|美和神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miwa Shrine (Nagano)|qq=}} * {{ill|Yufuku Shrine|ja|湯福神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yufuku Shrine|1=Yubuku Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11563772}} ※ * '''Takei Shrine''' ※ * {{ill|Tsuma Shrine|ja|妻科神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuma Shrine|qq=}} ※ * {{ill|Kamo Shrine (Nagano)|ja|加茂神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamo Shrine (Nagano)|1=Kamo Shrine (disambiguation)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11398885}} * {{ill|Kidome Shrine|ja|木留神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kidome Shrine|1=Kitome Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11519306}} * {{ill|Yanagihara Shrine (Nagano)|ja|柳原神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yanagihara Shrine (Nagano)|1=Yanagihara Shrine (Nagano)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q131226981}}

Although the shrine was destroyed in the Zenkoji Earthquake, this large votive plaque depicts the Onbashira Festival procession held during the shrine’s reconstruction 13 years later.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Nagano City Cultural Property Database |url=http://bunkazai-nagano.jp/modules/dbsearch/page1176.html |website=bunkazai-nagano.jp |access-date=2023-03-12}}</ref>
== References == {{Reflist}} == See Also == * [[:en:List of Shinto shrines in Japan|List of Shinto shrines in Japan]]

[[ja:武井神社]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Nagano]] [[Category:Son-sha in Nagano Prefecture]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> {{wikidata link|Q11545788}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:武井神社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:武井神社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:武井神社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[da:d:Q11545788]] Wikidata name: Takei Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:29, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:46, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | Name=Takei Shrine | image=File:Takei Shrine.jpg | location=[[:en:Nagano (city)|Nagano, Nagano]], [[:en:Nagano Prefecture|Nagano Prefecture]], Higashimachi 191 | coordinates={{Coord|36.6565194|N|138.1895556|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deity={{ill|Takeminakata|WD=Q9150391}} | Status=Village shrine | Main Hall= | Alternate Names= | Annual Festival= | prefecture={{ill|Nagano|WD=Q11654093}} }}
'''Takei Shrine''' (武井神社) is located in {{ill|Higashimachi, Nagano|ja|長野市東町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashimachi, Nagano|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, [[:en:Nagano (city)|Nagano City]], [[:en:Nagano Prefecture|Nagano Prefecture]]. Along with {{ill|Tsuma Shrine|ja|妻科神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuma Shrine|qq=}} and {{ill|Yufuku Shrine|ja|湯福神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yufuku Shrine|1=Yubuku Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11563772}}, it is known as one of the "Three Shrines of Zenkoji" (善光寺三社, also called the "Three Guardian Shrines of Zenkoji"). The exact founding date is unclear, but oral tradition suggests it may date back to the 5th year of Empress Jitō's reign (692 CE). The two deities enshrined together were added later.
== Overview ==
The origin of the shrine's name is explained in two theories: one from the {{ill|Shinano Hōkan|ja|信濃宝鑑|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinano Hōkan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which states that the land belonging to [[:en:Suwa-taisha|Suwa-taisha]] was called "Takei" (or "Takei" written as 武居), and since the same deities were enshrined, it became known as Takei Shrine. Another theory claims that the shrine was established by the Takei Hōri clan (武居祝, pronounced as "Takei Hōri"), a family of Shinto priests. In ancient times, the shrine was referred to as Takei Myōjin or Suwa Myōjin. The current name was adopted in the 4th year of the Bunka era (1807).
The area where Takei Shrine is located was referred to as "Takei" in old maps, with the road to the south labeled as "Takei Alley" (武井小路).
There used to be an associated shrine to the north, known as Takei Ebisu Shrine, which is now [[:en:Nishinomiya Shrine|Nishinomiya Shrine]].<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|title=Nagano City History Vol. 8 |date=1997-10-16 |publisher=Tokyo Hōrei Publishing |page=42}}</ref>
== Shrine Grounds ==
The shrine grounds were expanded in 1897 (Meiji 30).<ref name=":0" />
=== Worship Hall ===
The current worship hall (拝殿) was rebuilt in 2009 (Heisei 21) as part of the commemorative project for the Onbashira Festival held the following year. The previous worship hall was constructed 13 years after the shrine buildings were destroyed in the major earthquake of 1847 (Kōka 4).<ref name=":0" />
[[File:武井神社 雷電の力石.jpg|thumb|Raiden's Power Stone]]
=== Raiden's Power Stone ===
According to tradition, there used to be a river southeast of the shrine with a bridge called Takei Bridge. When people struggled to move the heavy stone bridge, the famous sumo wrestler {{ill|Raiden Tameemon|ja|雷電為右衛門|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Raiden Tameemon|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 雷電爲右エ門|1=Raiden Tameemon|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q707985}}, who was on tour in Zenkoji, happened to pass by and moved the stone with ease.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Legends of the Temple Town |date=2003-04-10 |publisher=Ryūhō Shobō |pages=131-133}}</ref> It is also said that if children stand on this stone, they will grow up healthy (as noted on the shrine’s information board).
[[File:矢島稲荷社 武井神社.jpg|thumb|Yajima Inari Shrine]]
=== Yajima Inari Shrine ===
Long ago, a blind man named Yajima Ichirōbee lived in Iwaki Town, north of the shrine. Every night when he went to practice the {{ill|biwa|ja|琵琶|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/biwa|1=pipa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6685124}}, someone would kindly guide him. One day, he discovered the corpse of a fox and realized that the person who had been helping him was, in fact, a fox. Ichirōbee respectfully buried the fox in his garden and enshrined it by building an {{ill|Inari Shrine|ja|稲荷社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inari Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 稲荷神社|1=Inari shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q514480}}. This happened in 1800 (Kansei 12). The shrine was later relocated to the grounds of Takei Shrine in 1896 (Meiji 29) and is now known as Yajima Inari Shrine.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Legends of the Temple Town |date=2003-04-10 |publisher=Ryūhō Shobō |pages=128-130}}</ref>
=== Matsuo Shrine ===
=== Konpira Shrine, Mitsumine Shrine, and Tenjin Shrine ===
=== Sarutahiko Ōkami Monument === <gallery> File:松尾社（武井神社）.jpg|Matsuo Shrine File:金毘羅社・三峰社・天神社（武井神社）.jpg|Konpira Shrine, Mitsumine Shrine, Tenjin Shrine File:猿田彦大神の塚（武井神社）.jpg|Sarutahiko Ōkami Monument </gallery>
== Rituals ==
=== Onbashira Festival === {{main|Onbashira Festival}} Held in the years of the Tiger and the Monkey, similar to the Onbashira Festival of Suwa Taisha. The shrines of Zenkoji—Takei Shrine, Tsuma Shrine, Yufuku Shrine—along with {{ill|Minochi Taisha|ja|水内大社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minochi Taisha|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, take turns hosting the festival.
=== Misayama Festival ===
On the night of August 26, sacred chopsticks made from [[:en:Miscanthus sinensis|Japanese pampas grass]] are offered at the altar to pray for children's healthy growth, household safety, and prosperous business. On the morning of August 27, eating red bean porridge with these sacred chopsticks is believed to bring health and protection for the year.
== Cultural Properties ==
; Designated Tangible Cultural Property of Nagano City * **Large Ema Painting of the Onbashira Festival Procession** - Painted in 1860 (Man'en 1). Although the shrine was destroyed in the Zenkoji Earthquake, this large votive plaque depicts the Onbashira Festival procession held during the shrine’s reconstruction 13 years later.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Nagano City Cultural Property Database |url=http://bunkazai-nagano.jp/modules/dbsearch/page1176.html |website=bunkazai-nagano.jp |access-date=2023-03-12}}</ref>
== References == {{Reflist}} == See Also == * [[:en:List of Shinto shrines in Japan|List of Shinto shrines in Japan]]
; Seven Shrines of Zenkoji : ※ indicates the Three Guardian Shrines of Zenkoji (善光寺三鎮守, Zenkoji Sansha) * {{ill|Miwa Shrine (Nagano)|ja|美和神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miwa Shrine (Nagano)|qq=}} * {{ill|Yufuku Shrine|ja|湯福神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yufuku Shrine|1=Yubuku Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11563772}} ※ * '''Takei Shrine''' ※ * {{ill|Tsuma Shrine|ja|妻科神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuma Shrine|qq=}} ※ * {{ill|Kamo Shrine (Nagano)|ja|加茂神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamo Shrine (Nagano)|1=Kamo Shrine (disambiguation)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11398885}} * {{ill|Kidome Shrine|ja|木留神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kidome Shrine|1=Kitome Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11519306}} * {{ill|Yanagihara Shrine (Nagano)|ja|柳原神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yanagihara Shrine (Nagano)|1=Yanagihara Shrine (Nagano)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q131226981}}

Ichirōbee respectfully buried the fox in his garden and enshrined it by building an {{ill|Inari Shrine|ja|稲荷社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inari Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 稲荷神社|1=Inari shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q514480}}. This happened in 1800 (Kansei 12). The shrine was later relocated to the grounds of Takei Shrine in 1896 (Meiji 29) and is now known as Yajima Inari Shrine.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Legends of the Temple Town |date=2003-04-10 |publisher=Ryūhō Shobō |pages=128-130}}</ref>
=== Matsuo Shrine ===
=== Konpira Shrine, Mitsumine Shrine, and Tenjin Shrine ===
=== Sarutahiko Ōkami Monument === <gallery> File:松尾社（武井神社）.jpg|Matsuo Shrine File:金毘羅社・三峰社・天神社（武井神社）.jpg|Konpira Shrine, Mitsumine Shrine, Tenjin Shrine File:猿田彦大神の塚（武井神社）.jpg|Sarutahiko Ōkami Monument </gallery>
== Rituals ==
=== Onbashira Festival === {{main|Onbashira Festival}} Held in the years of the Tiger and the Monkey, similar to the Onbashira Festival of Suwa Taisha. The shrines of Zenkoji—Takei Shrine, Tsuma Shrine, Yufuku Shrine—along with {{ill|Minochi Taisha|ja|水内大社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minochi Taisha|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, take turns hosting the festival.
=== Misayama Festival ===
On the night of August 26, sacred chopsticks made from [[:en:Miscanthus sinensis|Japanese pampas grass]] are offered at the altar to pray for children's healthy growth, household safety, and prosperous business. On the morning of August 27, eating red bean porridge with these sacred chopsticks is believed to bring health and protection for the year.
== Cultural Properties ==
; Designated Tangible Cultural Property of Nagano City * **Large Ema Painting of the Onbashira Festival Procession** - Painted in 1860 (Man'en 1). Although the shrine was destroyed in the Zenkoji Earthquake, this large votive plaque depicts the Onbashira Festival procession held during the shrine’s reconstruction 13 years later.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Nagano City Cultural Property Database |url=http://bunkazai-nagano.jp/modules/dbsearch/page1176.html |website=bunkazai-nagano.jp |access-date=2023-03-12}}</ref>
== References == {{Reflist}} == See Also == * [[:en:List of Shinto shrines in Japan|List of Shinto shrines in Japan]]
; Seven Shrines of Zenkoji : ※ indicates the Three Guardian Shrines of Zenkoji (善光寺三鎮守, Zenkoji Sansha) * {{ill|Miwa Shrine (Nagano)|ja|美和神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miwa Shrine (Nagano)|qq=}} * {{ill|Yufuku Shrine|ja|湯福神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yufuku Shrine|1=Yubuku Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11563772}} ※ * '''Takei Shrine''' ※ * {{ill|Tsuma Shrine|ja|妻科神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuma Shrine|qq=}} ※ * {{ill|Kamo Shrine (Nagano)|ja|加茂神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamo Shrine (Nagano)|1=Kamo Shrine (disambiguation)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11398885}} * {{ill|Kidome Shrine|ja|木留神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kidome Shrine|1=Kitome Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11519306}} * {{ill|Yanagihara Shrine (Nagano)|ja|柳原神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yanagihara Shrine (Nagano)|1=Yanagihara Shrine (Nagano)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q131226981}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:武井神社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:武井神社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:武井神社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11545788]] Wikidata name: Takei Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:29, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:46, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | Name=Takei Shrine | image=File:Takei Shrine.jpg | location=[[:en:Nagano (city)|Nagano, Nagano]], [[:en:Nagano Prefecture|Nagano Prefecture]], Higashimachi 191 | coordinates={{Coord|36.6565194|N|138.1895556|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deity={{ill|Takeminakata|WD=Q9150391}} | Status=Village shrine | Main Hall= | Alternate Names= | Annual Festival= | prefecture={{ill|Nagano|WD=Q11654093}} }}
'''Takei Shrine''' (武井神社) is located in {{ill|Higashimachi, Nagano|ja|長野市東町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashimachi, Nagano|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, [[:en:Nagano (city)|Nagano City]], [[:en:Nagano Prefecture|Nagano Prefecture]]. Along with {{ill|Tsuma Shrine|ja|妻科神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuma Shrine|qq=}} and {{ill|Yufuku Shrine|ja|湯福神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yufuku Shrine|1=Yubuku Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11563772}}, it is known as one of the "Three Shrines of Zenkoji" (善光寺三社, also called the "Three Guardian Shrines of Zenkoji"). The exact founding date is unclear, but oral tradition suggests it may date back to the 5th year of Empress Jitō's reign (692 CE). The two deities enshrined together were added later.
== Overview ==
The origin of the shrine's name is explained in two theories: one from the {{ill|Shinano Hōkan|ja|信濃宝鑑|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinano Hōkan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which states that the land belonging to [[:en:Suwa-taisha|Suwa-taisha]] was called "Takei" (or "Takei" written as 武居), and since the same deities were enshrined, it became known as Takei Shrine. Another theory claims that the shrine was established by the Takei Hōri clan (武居祝, pronounced as "Takei Hōri"), a family of Shinto priests. In ancient times, the shrine was referred to as Takei Myōjin or Suwa Myōjin. The current name was adopted in the 4th year of the Bunka era (1807).
The area where Takei Shrine is located was referred to as "Takei" in old maps, with the road to the south labeled as "Takei Alley" (武井小路).
There used to be an associated shrine to the north, known as Takei Ebisu Shrine, which is now [[:en:Nishinomiya Shrine|Nishinomiya Shrine]].<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|title=Nagano City History Vol. 8 |date=1997-10-16 |publisher=Tokyo Hōrei Publishing |page=42}}</ref>
== Shrine Grounds ==
The shrine grounds were expanded in 1897 (Meiji 30).<ref name=":0" />
=== Worship Hall ===
The current worship hall (拝殿) was rebuilt in 2009 (Heisei 21) as part of the commemorative project for the Onbashira Festival held the following year. The previous worship hall was constructed 13 years after the shrine buildings were destroyed in the major earthquake of 1847 (Kōka 4).<ref name=":0" />
[[File:武井神社 雷電の力石.jpg|thumb|Raiden's Power Stone]]
=== Raiden's Power Stone ===
According to tradition, there used to be a river southeast of the shrine with a bridge called Takei Bridge. When people struggled to move the heavy stone bridge, the famous sumo wrestler {{ill|Raiden Tameemon|ja|雷電為右衛門|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Raiden Tameemon|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 雷電爲右エ門|1=Raiden Tameemon|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q707985}}, who was on tour in Zenkoji, happened to pass by and moved the stone with ease.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Legends of the Temple Town |date=2003-04-10 |publisher=Ryūhō Shobō |pages=131-133}}</ref> It is also said that if children stand on this stone, they will grow up healthy (as noted on the shrine’s information board).
[[File:矢島稲荷社 武井神社.jpg|thumb|Yajima Inari Shrine]]
=== Yajima Inari Shrine ===
Long ago, a blind man named Yajima Ichirōbee lived in Iwaki Town, north of the shrine. Every night when he went to practice the {{ill|biwa|ja|琵琶|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/biwa|1=pipa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6685124}}, someone would kindly guide him. One day, he discovered the corpse of a fox and realized that the person who had been helping him was, in fact, a fox. Ichirōbee respectfully buried the fox in his garden and enshrined it by building an {{ill|Inari Shrine|ja|稲荷社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inari Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 稲荷神社|1=Inari shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q514480}}. This happened in 1800 (Kansei 12). The shrine was later relocated to the grounds of Takei Shrine in 1896 (Meiji 29) and is now known as Yajima Inari Shrine.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Legends of the Temple Town |date=2003-04-10 |publisher=Ryūhō Shobō |pages=128-130}}</ref>
=== Matsuo Shrine ===
=== Konpira Shrine, Mitsumine Shrine, and Tenjin Shrine ===
=== Sarutahiko Ōkami Monument === <gallery> File:松尾社（武井神社）.jpg|Matsuo Shrine File:金毘羅社・三峰社・天神社（武井神社）.jpg|Konpira Shrine, Mitsumine Shrine, Tenjin Shrine File:猿田彦大神の塚（武井神社）.jpg|Sarutahiko Ōkami Monument </gallery>
== Rituals ==

The shrines of Zenkoji—Takei Shrine, Tsuma Shrine, Yufuku Shrine—along with {{ill|Minochi Taisha|ja|水内大社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minochi Taisha|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, take turns hosting the festival.
=== Misayama Festival ===
On the night of August 26, sacred chopsticks made from [[:en:Miscanthus sinensis|Japanese pampas grass]] are offered at the altar to pray for children's healthy growth, household safety, and prosperous business. On the morning of August 27, eating red bean porridge with these sacred chopsticks is believed to bring health and protection for the year.
== Cultural Properties ==
; Designated Tangible Cultural Property of Nagano City * **Large Ema Painting of the Onbashira Festival Procession** - Painted in 1860 (Man'en 1). Although the shrine was destroyed in the Zenkoji Earthquake, this large votive plaque depicts the Onbashira Festival procession held during the shrine’s reconstruction 13 years later.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Nagano City Cultural Property Database |url=http://bunkazai-nagano.jp/modules/dbsearch/page1176.html |website=bunkazai-nagano.jp |access-date=2023-03-12}}</ref>
